coredata.dc:title: Cost-effective and facile fluorescent probes for label-free recognition of chlorpromazine hydrochloride and logic gate operation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Limei
Qin, Ju
Yang, Qiang
Wei, Siqi
Yang, Rui
coredata.dc:description: Herein, water-soluble nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) possessing strong blue fluorescence were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal treatment using citric acid monohydrate(CA)and semicarbazide hydrochloride with NaOH as solvent. What's important in this article is that the N-CDs interacted with chlorpromazine hydrochloride (CPH), leading to the quenching of strong fluorescence. Based on the above phenomenon, a “switch-off” fluorescent probe with excellent sensitivity and superior selectivity was designed for specifically detecting CPH, and an “AND” logic gate for sensing CPH was first proposed. In this system, two linear ranges of 7.5–100.0 μM and 100.0–1000.0 μM CPH could be easily tracked, and a 0.043 μM detection limit was available. Additionally, the as-constructed sensor was conditioned for CPH determination in spiked human urine with satisfactory results, proving this is a potential and promising strategy for rapid screening of CPH in biological fluids. According to what we know, it is the first research regarding the use of CDs without any material decoration as probes to test CPH, which opens up the optical path for CPH detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Chlorpromazine hydrochloride
Fluorescence quenching
Logic gate

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Special Fund of Chongqing Key Laboratory (CSTC) . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The TEM image of N-CDs. (b) the size distribution of N-CDs. (c) FTIR spectra of N-CDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence excitation (Ex=340nm) and emission (Em=427nm) of N-CDs. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra corresponding to different excitation wavelengths.

(a) Emission spectra of N-CDs at different reaction temperatures. (b) at different mass ratios between CA and semicarbazide hydrochloride. (c) at different reaction time. (d) at different concentrations of NaOH. (e)The effect of pH on N-CDs and the developed system.
(a) The stability of N-CDs (b) the effect of various NaCl concentrations on N-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs at different CPH concentrations.(b) Calibration chart for N-CDs + CPH system. (c) The relationship between ΔF and CPH from 7.5 to 100.0 μM. (d) from 100.0 to 1000.0 μM (pH=7.0).

Selective fluorescence response of N-CDs toward (a) 200 μL 10.0 mM CPH and various interferences (1:blank; 2:NO3 −; 3: CH3COO- ; 4:SO4 2-; 5: F−; 6: I−; 7: H2PO4 −; 8:CN- ; 9: HCO3- ; 10:C2O4 2-;11: Cl−; 12: ClO4- ; 13: Al3+; 14: Cd2+; 15: Fe2+; 16: Cu2+; 17: Hg2+; 18: Ni2+; 19: Pb2+; 20: Ag+; 21: Cys; 22: Lys; 23: Phe; 24: His; 25: Arg; 26:Try ;27:Glu; 28: lactose; 29: maltose; 30: UA; 31: AA; 32: Thiamine; 33:Ca2+; 34:Zn2+; 35:Fe3+ (b) 200 μL 10.0 mM phenothiazines.).

(a) The Stern–Volmer curves for CPH quenching N-CDs at different temperatures. C(CPH): 7.5; 37.5; 50; 80;100 μM. (b) Time-resolved fluorescence decays of N-CDs and N-CDs + CPH system. C(CPH) :1 mM, pH = 7.0. (c) Absorption spectra of N-CDs + CPH, N-CDs and CPH.
Truth table for the “AND” logic gate.
Schematic diagram about formation process of N-CDs and detection principle for CPH.
Comparison of different methods for detecting CPH.

row.entry: sensitive, time-consuming, multi-step operation, instrument complex
high separation efficiency, complicated operation, expensive instruments
facile, wide linear range, eco–friendly, economical sensitive, effective

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: HPLC*: high performance liquid chromatography; UPLC-ES(+)MS/MS*: an ultra performance liquid chromatography coupled to an electrospray tandem triple quadrupole mass spectrometer.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results for the detection of CPH in spiked urine.

head.ce:title: Cost-effective and facile fluorescent probes for label-free recognition of chlorpromazine hydrochloride and logic gate operation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analysis, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analysis
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, water-soluble nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) possessing strong blue fluorescence were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal treatment using citric acid monohydrate(CA)and semicarbazide hydrochloride with NaOH as solvent. What's important in this article is that the N-CDs interacted with chlorpromazine hydrochloride (CPH), leading to the quenching of strong fluorescence. Based on the above phenomenon, a “switch-off” fluorescent probe with excellent sensitivity and superior selectivity was designed for specifically detecting CPH, and an “AND” logic gate for sensing CPH was first proposed. In this system, two linear ranges of 7.5–100.0 μM and 100.0–1000.0 μM CPH could be easily tracked, and a 0.043 μM detection limit was available. Additionally, the as-constructed sensor was conditioned for CPH determination in spiked human urine with satisfactory results, proving this is a potential and promising strategy for rapid screening of CPH in biological fluids. According to what we know, it is the first research regarding the use of CDs without any material decoration as probes to test CPH, which opens up the optical path for CPH detection.

ce:section.ce:para: As being well known, chlorpromazine hydrochloride (CPH), is a phenothiazine drug containing an aliphatic side chain, which is beneficial for treating psychiatric patients suffering from depression [1]. It has the effects of controlling excitement, hyperkinetic symptoms, anxiety and other psychomotor disorders of persons with schizophrenia and reducing the manic phase of manic depressive states [2]. CPH is also applied to prevent vomiting and intravenous anti-hypertensive therapeutics [3].

Currently, the methods available for determining CPH in pure solution or pharmaceutical formulations were described in scores of literatures involving spectrophotometry [4–6], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7–9], differential pulse [10], electrochemical methods [11], atomic absorption spectrometry [12], chemiluminescence [13]. Unfortunately, the reported methods mentioned above are endowed with some defects, such as high-cost, sophisticated operating steps and time consumption. In addition, considering the importance of practical applications, these systems cannot be miniaturized, thus leading to lacking the ability to carry on real-time analysis. Accordingly, it is a highlight to establish a rapid and facile method to measure CPH in both pharmaceutical formulations and body fluids.

More recently, a burgeoning category of prominent substitute, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), were fabricated by Xu's group in 2004 [14], which have caught an apparent focus of attention in the fields of fundamental research and technical applications in recent years [15] owing to massive significant intrinsic features in terms of low toxicity, fabrication simplicity, excellent sensitivity and satisfactory selectivity [16–18]. Moreover, CDs emerge as a newly rising start of carbon-based nanomaterials with average diameter below 10 nm, enjoying exceptional water and organic solvents dispersibility [14,17,19]. It is found that CDs are mostly prepared through two major categories that one is “bottom-up” strategies by using a variety of polymers or organic molecules precursors and the other is “top-down” approaches by decomposing different carbon materials into single nanodots [20–23]. Compared with the “top-down” methods, the “bottom-up” ways possess massive appealing properties in the synthesis of superior CDs, like simple fabrication procedures, economical raw materials. Inspired by the above information, CDs have been regarded as attractive candidates in a myriad of applications including imaging, medical diagnosis, photocatalysis and drug delivery [24–26]. To date, the CDs as fluorescent probes have been also extensively applied to detect proteins, numerous ions and small molecules, etc [27–29]. However, some of the above preparations generally require costly materials, stringent synthetic conditions or complicated purification procedures, which restrict the further application of CDs in diverse areas of analysis. Herein, to address these limitations, a facile and approachable synthetic route to synthesize CDs is still a pressing need.

In this report, we here constructed a new, cost effective and facile preparative route toward N-CDs originating from CA and semicarbazide hydrochloride with NaOH as solvent, which were successfully evaluated as shockingly effective fluorescent probes for CPH detection (scheme 1 ). In this strategy, 7.5–100.0 μM and 100.0–1000.0 μM CPH could be easily detected with a detection limit of 0.043 μM. It is worth mentioning that no literature has been reported to use CDs without any material modification as probes to detect CPH. The strategy has been demonstrated to possess distinguished performances, like preparation simplicity, wider linear ranges. Furthermore, the use of non-toxic materials to prepare N-CDs through the “bottom-up” approach made the probes more environmentally friendly, which was pave the way to implement N-CDs as fluorescent probes for practical applications. In addition, the method was first applied to design a simple “AND” logic gate. Most significantly, the proposed method greatly supported its potential application for detecting CPH in human urine samples with satisfactory results.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA) was supplied by Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Plant (Chengdu, China). Semicarbazide hydrochloride was purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai, Co., Ltd. China). The Britton-Robison(BR)buffer solution was obtained by mixing three acids (consisted of 2.36 mL HAc, 2.71 mL H3PO4, and 2.47 g H3BO3) with 0.2 M NaOH in a proportion. All reagents were of analytical reagent grade, and ultrapure water (18.2 Ω.cm.) was supplied throughout the experiment.

All fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). A UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was used to measure the UV absorption spectra. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, was performed to acquire the morphology and size of the as-prepared N-CDs. Functional groups analysis were carried out on a FTIR-8400S Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Kyoto, Japan, FTIR). Fluorescence emission decay curves of system and N-CDs were tested by an FL-TCSPC Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrometer (France, Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc). A pHS–3D pH meter (Shanghai, China) was employed to detect the pH value. N-CDs were synthesized by a Teflon-lined autoclave (Chongqing, China). Ultrapure water was produced by an ATS16-1130-072 ultrapure water producing device(Chongqing, China).

CDs were prepared according to previously reported articles with some modifications [30–32]. Briefly, CA (0.5 g) and semicarbazide hydrochloride (0.25 g) were dissolved in NaOH (0.3 M 5 mL) with ultrasonication for 10 min. Then, the mixed solution was transferred to 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, followed by heating at 200 °C for 6 h. After the mixture cooled down to room temperature, the reaction solution was added into 20 mL ultrapure water and purified with a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The purified product, a faint yellow and transparent solution, was subjected to dialyze in the dialysis bag, whose molecular weight cut off (MWCO) was 500, against ultrapure water for 12 h to eliminate the undissolved substance. Finally, the resultant faint yellow solution was stored at 4 °C for further research.

The process of CPH selective determination was described at room temperature and as follows: into a 2 mL centrifuge tube was sequentially transferred 20 μL N-CDs, 50 μL BR buffer (pH 7.0), and various concentrations of CPH. Subsequently, the resulting mixed solution was diluted to the mark with ultrapure water and well mixed at room temperature (20 ± 5 ℃). Finally, the fluorescence emission spectra were acquired after incubation for 10 min under the excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

The quantum yield (QY) of the N-CDs was obtained by comparing the integrated fluorescence intensities (excited at 340 nm) and absorption values (at 340 nm) of N-CDs samples with quinine sulfate's. Quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (Φs = 54.2％) was chosen as a reference standard, and the N-CDs were dissolved in water. The quantum yield of the N-CDs was calculated using the following formula [33]:
Φ u
=
Φ s
F u
F s
A s
A u

in which Φu represented the QY of N-CDs. Fu /Fs denoted the areas under the fluorescence curves of N-CDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. As /Au were absorbance of the standard and sample at the 340 nm excitation accordingly. The QY of N-CDs measured with the reference method was found to be 8.96%. (Fig. 1 ).

To discuss and analyze the notable features of N-CDs, characterizations like TEM, XPS and FTIR were recorded. With as-fabricated CDs in hand, the TEM and size distribution were illustrated in Fig. 2 . It clearly revealed that the as-prepared N-CDs were spherical particles (Fig. 2a) with the mean diameters about 2.35 nm by measuring 150 nanoparticles (Fig. 2b). Simultaneously, the TEM image presented a clear crystalline structure, corresponding to lattice spacing of 0.20 nm (inset in Fig. 2a), which attributed to the (102) diffraction planes of graphitic (sp2) carbon [26]. From all the discussion presented above, the N-CDs of high crystallinity were successfully yielded by the “bottom-up” method.

Besides what was mentioned above, the chemical structure of the as-produced N-CDs was further evidenced by FTIR. As detailed in Fig. 2c, the acquired N-CDs demonstrated characteristic absorption of OH/NH stretching vibrations at 3433 cm−1, CO stretching vibrations at 1662 and 1579 cm−1, CN stretching vibrations at 1440 cm−1, CH bending vibrations at 1300 cm−1, CO stretching vibrations at 1209, 1159 and 1012 cm−1. Together, these results unambiguously implied that semicarbazide hydrochloride as the nitrogen source has successfully adulterated into the N-CDs. Along with that, plentiful hydrophilic functional groups existed on the surface of N-CDs, which result in exceptional water solubility of N-CDs and can afford the N-CDs as distinctive probes for CPH investigation. Furthermore, the components and structures of the produced N-CDs were characterized by XPS. The XPS spectra and related results and discussion were shown in Supplementary material.

To inquire into the optical properties of N-CDs, the UV–vis absorption spectrum and the excitation and emission spectrum of the N-CDs were made through a thorough inquiry. The resultant N-CDs have no apparent absorption but a broad UV–vis absorption, ascribed to the n-π* transition of N-CDs [34]. From the inset of Fig. 3 a, a fluid that is faint yellow under daylight and exhibits bright blue luminescence under UV light is observed. As described in Fig. 3b, when the excitation wavelengths were increased from 300 to 420 nm, the emission peak of N-CDs was red shifted from 422 to 482 nm, resulting from the different particle sizes and considerable distribution of diverse surface states [35,36]. In other words, the N-CDs prepared via the “bottom-up” method possessed wavelength-dependent fluorescent emission behaviors. Besides, a maximum was situated in 427 nm with the strongest intensity of N-CDs when excited at 340 nm.

In order to obtain N-CDs with favorable fluorescence properties, a great many factors which would affect the synthesis reaction, such as reaction temperature, mass ratio, reaction time and NaOH concentration, were investigated (Supplementary Material). As the temperature increased, the reaction was more complete, thereby increasing the yield of N-CDs in Fig. 4 a. As displayed in Fig. 4b, mass ratio had a pivotal impact on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs, so the mass ratio of CA to semicarbazide hydrochloride was strictly controlled at 2:1. Meanwhile, the reaction time was explored under the same temperature, mass ratio and NaOH concentration, and the optimal reaction time was 6 h in Fig. 4c. As clearly seen from Fig. 4d, when NaOH concentration was 0.3 M, the fluorescence intensity was the strongest. In a word, the optimum synthesis conditions of N-CDs were selected as follows: the reaction temperature and the reaction time were 200 °C and 6 h, respectively, and the mass ratio of CA to semicarbazide hydrochloride was 2:1 with 0.3 M NaOH.

With better sensing behaviors in mind, the pH values were further assessed. It was easy to draw conclusions that Fig. 4e reflected the intensity of fluorescence of N-CDs was increasing as the pH value rose from 2.0 to 5.0. Under acidic conditions, hydrogen bonds were easily formed between the N-CDs, resulting in fluorescence quenching. However, the N-CDs were substantially monodisperse under alkaline conditions, making the fluorescence intensity almost stable [37]. Then the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs kept constant, which pH ranging from 6.0 to 11.0. The as-described system held a pH-independent feature in which the fluorescence intensity almost remained stable within 2.0–11.0 pH range. Note that ΔF was few fluctuations at pH 6.0 to 11.0. Finally, pH 7.0 similar to the physiological pH environment was introduced for the subsequent experiments.

For nanomaterials, the stability was one of manipulative contributing factors that made chemical detection or biological sensor more accurate. No perceptible photobleaching was viewed after continuous radiation for 1 h under the UV lamp (Fig. 5 a). Moreover, the fluorescence response of N-CDs under high salt conditions was also surveyed. One can see from Fig. 5b that the tested NaCl had no significant fluorescence quenching effect even if the concentration of NaCl solution was as high as 2.5 M, indicating that the N-CDs can be applied to more complicated conditions.

For one thing, the sensitivity of as-established platform to the quantitative determination of CPH was evaluated. Multiple concentrations of CPH were added into the N-CDs solution under optimal conditions, and then fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs were recorded. As revealed in Fig. 6 a, it came to our attention that the fluorescence quenching was linearly dependent on the increasing concentrations of CPH. As demonstrated from Fig. 6c, good linearity was found with CPH concentrations in the range of 7.5 to 100.0 μM (R2 = 0.9926), along with the following correlation equation:ΔF = 1.03178c+6.767 (ΔF=F-F 0, where F and F 0 referred to the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs in the absence and presence of CPH, respectively; c referred to the concentration of CPH). The results were deposited in Fig. 6d, and a good linear correlation (ΔF = 0.0079c+5.77) over the range from 100.0 to 1000.0 μM (R 2 = 0.9955) was achieved. The limit of detection (LOD) was 43.0 nM. (LOD = 3σ/S, σ is the relative standard deviation of N-CDs blank solution (N = 10) and S is the slope of the calibration curve [38]) The possible reason for the different slopes is that the combination mode is different. From the job plots (Fig.S2), it can be clearly seen that the CPH and N-CDs have a combination ratio of 1:1 at low concentrations and 2:1 at high concentrations. The sensing platform of previously published strategies for CPH detection were shown in Table 1 . In comparison with the existing assays, the proposed method here had no material decoration and provided a superior linear range, explaining that the approach was feasible and reliable for sensitively detecting CPH.

More importantly, the selectivity was another decisive factor to evaluate the capability of a strategy due to the complexity of the real samples. Thus, various common species, especially in the human body, including (10.0 mM) anion ions (NO3 −, CH3COO−, SO4 2-, F-, I−, H2PO4 −, CN−, Cl−,

HCO3 −, C2O4 2-, ClO4 −), mental ions (Al3+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Cu2+) and others (Cys, Lys, Phe, His, Arg, Tyr, Glu, AA, lactose, maltose, UA, Thiamine) were assessed. Except for CPH, the effects of other interferences on fluorescence were negligible in Fig. 7 a. As mentioned above, it clearly showed the N-CDs as a significantly sensitive chemosensor were competent for resisting interferents. Additionally, taking other phenothiazines (promethazine hydrochloride (PH), trifluoperazinedihydrochloride (TFPH), triflupromazine hydrochloride (TPH), thioproperazine mesylate (TPPM), thiolizine hydrochloride(TH), prochlorperazine dimaleate (PCPM)) into account may be capable of obstructing for detection of CPH, so their effects toward CPH were deeply investigated. Fig. 7b depicted these interferences could be almost ignored. The obtained outcomes manifested that the sensing system was highly selective and precise for CPH detection.

According to what we learn, fluorescence quenching of N-CDs arises from static quenching or dynamic quenching [44]. By calculating Ksv at different temperatures is a way to distinguish quenching types described above. When the Ksv is proportional to temperature, the quenching mechanism is dynamic. Otherwise, it is static [45]. As a consequence, to gain insight into the fluorescence quenching mechanism, Ksv at two temperatures (279 K, 303 K) was calculated based on the Stern–Volmer equation [46,47]:
F 0
/
F = 1 + K q
τ 0
Q
= 1 +
K
s v
Q
Where F and F0 refer to the fluorescence figures in the presence and absence of the quencher, respectively. K sv represents the Stern–Volmer quenching constant and [Q] is the concentration of quencher. τ
0
means the life time of fluorophore. Kq (K sv/ τ
0 ) stands for quenching rate constant. From Fig. 8 , the higher the temperature rose, the smaller K sv became, indicating that the quenching process may be static quenching.

In support of our previous speculation, the fluorescence lifetime was measured to assure the quenching mechanism. For the dynamic quenching, τ
0 /
τ
=
F0/F, while for the static quenching, τ
0 /
τ =1, the fluorescence lifetime of excited fluorescence molecules does not alter with the change of the quenching medium [48]. Fig. 8b displayed the fluorescence decays of N-CDs before and after introducing CPH, from which it could see that the lifetime of N-CDs and N-CDs + CPH presented no obvious changes, from 4.47 ns to 4.72 ns. The above-mentioned analysis implied the mechanism was static quenching.

Ultimately, to further elucidate our mechanism, the UV–vis spectroscopy was discussed. According to some previous literatures, we can clearly know that dynamic quenching is caused by collisional reactions, while static quenching is due to the formation of ground state complexes [49–51]. It showed the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CPH added to the solution was different from that of the without CPH in Fig. 8c. Consequently, we ensure that the aforementioned phenomenon is due to the fact that the ground state complex formed by the collision reaction does not change the absorption spectrum of the fluorophore. That is, the mechanism of the quenching process was certainly static.

To present the outstanding perspective of the developed approach in real samples, the as-presented N-CDs were employed to test CPH in human urine (Table 2 ). Urine samples were acquired from the healthy volunteers. Urine samples demanded to be centrifugated at 1500 rpm for 20 min, 1 mL supernatant was injected into a 50 mL volumetric flask, rinsed with water to the mark for further use. The CPH standard solution was added to the urine samples prior to analysis. As listed in Table 2, it demonstrated results for the detection of CPH in spiked urine, summarizing that recoveries of the known spiked amounts of CPH in urine are 96.12–100.1% with RSD around 1.26–3.02%. In spite of slight changes for CPH in biological fluids, around 80% of CPH excreted were unchanged in the urine [52]. From all the above discussion, there was convincing evidence supporting the approach to be reasonable for detecting CPH in human urine, which suggested the validity in practical applications of the presented strategy.

According to the phenomenon based on the quenching of the fluorescence of N-CDs in the presence of CPH, a simple “AND” logic gate was designed. The N-CDs and CPH were chosen as inputs and the fluorescence intensity at 427 nm as the outputs. For input, the presence and absence of N-CDs and CPH was defined as “1″ state and “0″ sate, respectively. For output listed in truth table (Fig. 9 ), they were defined as “1″ and “0″ corresponding to weak and strong fluorescence. Only with the input of N-CDs and CPH, the output was “1″ and the weak fluorescence was generated. In all other states, the outputs were an output signal of “0″ and the fluorescence was strong. Consequently, it could be found the gate was activated only when two inputs were provided [53].

In summary, we have creatively established a distinctive “switch-off” fluorescent probe for the detection of CPH on the basis of N-CDs fabricated by the facile, low-cost and straightforward hydrothermal treatment. The practical application of this developed sensor for sensing CPH has been testified in human urine samples with satisfactory results. As expected, the N-CDs are well suited for CPH detection in real samples. In this method, the limit of detection for selective determination of CPH was 0.043 μM, along with two linear ranges of 7.5–100.0 μM and 100.0–1000.0 μM. More attractively, an “AND” logic gate was successfully designed. Most impressively, this is the first report on the direct use CDs as probes to detect CPH, which provides an alternative example for the construction of the sensing system to detect CPH in the field of biological fluids and other practical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Special Fund of Chongqing Key Laboratory (CSTC).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2019.111918.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
